title: Severe Bleeding Control
summary: Immediate action to stop life-threatening bleeding using direct pressure or a tourniquet.
steps: 1. Identify the source of bleeding.
2. Apply firm, steady direct pressure using a clean cloth or gauze.
3. If bleeding is on a limb and life-threatening, apply a tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound (between the wound and the heart).
4. Tighten the tourniquet until bleeding stops completely.
5. Note the time the tourniquet was applied.
warnings: Do NOT remove the cloth if soaked; add more on top.
Do NOT loosen a tourniquet once applied; it must be removed by medical professionals.
A tourniquet is painful but necessary for life-threatening limb bleeding.
whenToSeekHelp: Always seek emergency medical help for severe bleeding, deep wounds, or if a tourniquet was used.

title: Burn Treatment
summary: Cool the burn and protect the skin from infection.
steps: 1. Stop the burning process (remove from heat source).
2. Cool the burn with cool (not cold/ice) running water for at least 10-20 minutes.
3. Remove jewelry or tight clothing before area swells.
4. Cover the burn loosely with a sterile, non-stick bandage or clean plastic wrap.
5. Keep the person warm to prevent shock.
warnings: Do NOT use ice, butter, or ointments on a fresh burn.
Do NOT pop blisters; they protect against infection.
whenToSeekHelp: Seek help if the burn is larger than the person's palm, on the face/hands/feet, or if it appears charred (white/black).

title: Fractures & Sprains
summary: Immobilize the injured area to prevent further damage.
steps: 1. Do not try to realign the bone.
2. Control any bleeding first.
3. Apply a splint to the area above and below the joint to keep it from moving.
4. Use padding (cloth, clothing) to make the splint comfortable.
5. Apply ice packs wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling (20 mins on/off).
warnings: Do NOT move the person if a spinal or neck injury is suspected.
Do NOT test the limb's ability to 'bear weight' if a break is suspected.
whenToSeekHelp: Seek immediate help if the bone is protruding, the limb is deformed, or there is loss of sensation/pulse below the injury.

title: Water Purification
summary: Methods to make found water safe for drinking in the wild.
steps: 1. Boil: Bring water to a rolling boil for at least 1 minute (3 mins at high altitude). This is the safest method.
2. Chemical: Use iodine or chlorine tablets according to package instructions.
3. Filtration: Use a portable filter with 0.1-0.2 micron pores to remove bacteria and protozoa.
4. Solar: In an emergency, leave clear water in a plastic bottle in direct sunlight for 6-8 hours (SODIS method).
warnings: Filtration alone may not remove viruses.
Avoid water with chemical smell or near industrial areas; these methods only kill biological threats.
whenToSeekHelp: Seek help if you experience severe vomiting, diarrhea, or dark urine after drinking untreated water.

title: Heat Exhaustion & Stroke
summary: Cool the body down rapidly to prevent organ damage.
steps: 1. Move to a cool, shaded area immediately.
2. Remove excess clothing.
3. Cool the person with water (spray, wet cloths) and fan them.
4. Apply ice packs to armpits, groin, and neck if available.
5. If conscious, give small sips of cool water or an electrolyte drink.
warnings: Heat stroke is a medical emergency. If the person is confused or unconscious, it is Heat Stroke.
Do NOT give fluids to an unconscious person.
whenToSeekHelp: Call for help immediately if the person has a high body temperature (>104F/40C), stops sweating, or becomes confused/seizure.

title: Hypothermia Treatment
summary: Restore body heat gradually and prevent further heat loss.
steps: 1. Move to a warm, dry shelter.
2. Replace wet clothing with dry clothes/blankets.
3. Warm the center of the body (chest, neck, groin) first.
4. Give warm, non-alcoholic, decaffeinated drinks if the person is conscious.
5. Use skin-to-skin contact if blankets are unavailable.
warnings: Do NOT rub or massage the person's limbs; this can cause cardiac arrest.
Do NOT use direct heat like hot water or heat lamps which can cause burns or shock.
whenToSeekHelp: Seek emergency help if shivering stops but the person remains cold, or if they become confused/drowsy.

title: Snake Bite Basics
summary: Minimize venom spread and get to a hospital fast.
steps: 1. Move away from the snake's striking distance.
2. Keep the person calm and minimize movement.
3. Keep the bite location at or slightly below the level of the heart.
4. Remove rings or tight clothing as swelling will occur.
5. Clean the wound gently but do not flush with water.
warnings: Do NOT try to catch the snake.
Do NOT use a tourniquet or ice.
Do NOT cut the wound or try to suck out the venom.
whenToSeekHelp: All snake bites should be treated as emergencies. Go to a hospital immediately.

title: Getting Lost Outdoors
summary: S.T.O.P strategy to survive and be found.
steps: 1. Sit: Sit down and calm yourself.
2. Think: How did you get here? What landmarks did you see?
3. Observe: Look for shelter, water, and potential hazards. Check your supplies.
4. Plan: Decide whether to stay put (recommended) or move. If staying, start preparing shelter and signals.
warnings: Staying put is almost always safer than wandering further into the unknown.
whenToSeekHelp: If you are not found by sunset, prepare for an overnight stay and prioritize warmth.

title: Signaling for Rescue
summary: Attract attention to your location using visual and auditory signals.
steps: 1. Ground Signals: Create a large 'X' or 'SOS' using rocks, logs, or digging in contrast with the ground.
2. Mirror: Use a signal mirror or any shiny object to flash sunlight toward planes or rescuers.
3. Fire: Build three fires in a triangle (international distress signal). Use green boughs for thick white smoke during the day.
4. Sound: Use a whistle (three blasts) or yell in groups of three.
warnings: Don't exhaust yourself yelling; a whistle is far more effective and less tiring.
whenToSeekHelp: Signal immediately if you see or hear potential rescuers.
